content.text: AWS Availability Zones are engineered as

. Physical separation, dedicated power infrastructure, independent networking, and the Nitro hypervisor combine to ensure that a failure in one AZ — whether caused by hardware, power, cooling, or a natural event — cannot propagate to any other AZ in the region.

attrs.alt: Physical isolation components of three AZs in us-east-1

content.text: Each AZ achieves fault isolation across four distinct dimensions. All four must hold simultaneously for true isolation:
: AZs within a region are typically ≥ 10 km apart, placing them in separate seismic fault zones, flood plains, and hurricane impact corridors.
: Separate utility feeds drawn from different grid substations, separate UPS banks, and dedicated backup generator sets. A grid substation failure affects at most one AZ.
: Computer Room Air Conditioner (CRAC) sets are dedicated per AZ or per data-center group. A cooling failure cascades within one AZ's physical boundary.

: Each AZ maintains its own BGP peering sessions and border routers. The redundant inter-AZ fiber backbone is separate from the intra-AZ fabric; inter-AZ traffic is charged precisely to create cost-awareness of cross-AZ calls.
AWS does not publish the precise addresses of its data centers, but the
) is a stable cross-account identifier that maps to a consistent physical location within those 10 km circles.
Understanding fault domains clarifies what each layer of redundancy buys you:
N+1 or 2N UPS and generator configuration
per AZ. The intent is that any single UPS module or generator can fail during a utility outage without dropping instances. Each AZ draws its utility power from:
Automatic transfer switches (ATS) that cut over to generator power within
— fast enough that most servers and hypervisors remain online without noticing.

Cooling systems follow the same independence principle. A thermal event (chiller failure, loss of cooling fluid) triggers alarms and automatic workload throttling within the affected AZ's thermal envelope but is contained by physical distance and dedicated piping.
Each AZ connects to the outside world through its own set of border routers running independent BGP sessions. The inter-AZ
carries VPC traffic between AZs within a region. This backbone is:
: Multiple fiber paths along different physical routes.
: Traffic traversing the inter-AZ backbone for Nitro-based instances is encrypted at the hardware layer.
: Round-trip latency within a region is typically 0.5–2 ms, enabling synchronous replication for services like RDS Multi-AZ and Aurora.
provides an additional layer of isolation within and across AZs. By offloading networking, storage, and security functions to dedicated Nitro cards:
— no AWS employee can log into the hypervisor to access customer workloads. This eliminates an entire class of insider-threat propagation.
hardware bug on one Nitro card
is bounded to that host and cannot affect other hosts, because each Nitro card is an independent NIC + I/O subsystem.
enforces hardware root of trust on each host during boot, rejecting firmware that has been tampered with.

# Verify your instance runs on Nitro (no 'Hypervisor' key = bare-metal or Nitro)
aws ec2 describe-instance-types \
  --instance-types m5.xlarge r6g.large c7g.2xlarge \
  --query 'InstanceTypes[*].{Type:InstanceType,Hypervisor:Hypervisor}' \
  --output table

# Nitro instances show no "xen" entry; bare-metal shows no entry at all
# Expected output for Nitro instances: Hypervisor = nitro
Redundancy Patterns Built on AZ Isolation
AZ isolation is only useful when your architecture is designed to run across multiple AZs. The following managed services either automatically span AZs or provide multi-AZ deployment options:
Synchronous standby in a different AZ
Data stored in ≥ 3 facilities per region
Tasks spread across AZs via service scheduler
EC2 Instance Spread and Placement Groups
For EC2, AZ isolation only protects against AZ-level events. To protect against rack-level failures within an AZ, use
Each instance on a separate physical rack/host
Small critical instance sets (Kafka brokers, ZooKeeper)
Groups of instances share a rack; groups are isolated
All instances packed on adjacent hardware
Low-latency HPC — trades resilience for performance

# Create a Spread placement group and launch two instances in different racks
aws ec2 create-placement-group \
  --group-name critical-3tier \
  --strategy spread

# Launch instances into the spread group (each lands on a distinct rack)
aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id ami-0abcdef1234567890 \
  --instance-type m5.xlarge \
  --count 3 \
  --subnet-id subnet-0abc123 \
  --placement GroupName=critical-3tier \
  --query 'Instances[*].{Id:InstanceId,AZ:Placement.AvailabilityZone}' \
  --output table

# Verify placement group details
aws ec2 describe-placement-groups \
  --group-names critical-3tier \
  --query 'PlacementGroups[0].{Name:GroupName,Strategy:Strategy,State:State}'
These design choices are frequently seen in real workloads and each eliminates the protection that AZ isolation provides:
All instances in a single AZ
AZ outage takes the entire application offline
EBS volume relied on across AZs
Volume cannot be attached in another AZ
Hardware failure requires manual restore from snapshot
ALB with targets only in one AZ
AZ outage removes all targets; ALB has no healthy hosts
Register targets in every AZ the ALB is in
Cache node failure means cold start for all traffic
Use cluster mode or replica in separate AZ
Application fails if that AZ is impaired
Use service endpoints (Route 53, ALB DNS) that reroute automatically
The only way to verify multi-AZ resilience is to
. AWS Fault Injection Service (FIS) provides managed AZ disruption experiments. A well-scoped FIS experiment terminates all EC2 instances in one AZ, simulating an AZ outage.
Because AZs fail independently, your observability stack must expose per-AZ metrics. The following CloudWatch metrics reveal AZ-specific degradation before it becomes a full outage:

import boto3

# Create a CloudWatch alarm for ALB HealthyHostCount per AZ
cw = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1')

def create_az_health_alarm(alb_name: str, az: str, min_healthy: int, sns_arn: str) -> str:
    alarm_name = f'{alb_name}-{az}-healthy-hosts'
    cw.put_metric_alarm(
        AlarmName=alarm_name,
        MetricName='HealthyHostCount',
        Namespace='AWS/ApplicationELB',
        Statistic='Average',
        Dimensions=[
            {'Name': 'LoadBalancer', 'Value': alb_name},
            {'Name': 'AvailabilityZone', 'Value': az},
        ],
        Period=60,
        EvaluationPeriods=2,
        Threshold=min_healthy,
        ComparisonOperator='LessThanThreshold',
        TreatMissingData='breaching',
        AlarmActions=[sns_arn],
        AlarmDescription=f'Healthy host count in {az} below {min_healthy}',
    )
    return alarm_name

# Create alarms for all three AZs
ALB = 'app/my-alb/50dc6c495c0c9188'
SNS = 'arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:ops-pager'

for az in ['us-east-1a', 'us-east-1b', 'us-east-1c']:
    name = create_az_health_alarm(ALB, az, min_healthy=1, sns_arn=SNS)
    print(f'Created alarm: {name}')

After deploying any service, confirm it is actually distributed across AZs — misconfiguration (e.g., forgetting to add subnets to an ALB, or launching an ASG into a single subnet) is the most common reason multi-AZ fails silently.

# Verify EC2 ASG is actually distributing across AZs
aws autoscaling describe-auto-scaling-groups \
  --auto-scaling-group-names my-web-asg \
  --query 'AutoScalingGroups[0].{AZs:AvailabilityZones,Subnets:VPCZoneIdentifier}' \
  --output json

# Confirm RDS is Multi-AZ
aws rds describe-db-instances \
  --db-instance-identifier prod-db \
  --query 'DBInstances[0].{MultiAZ:MultiAZ,SecondaryAZ:SecondaryAvailabilityZone,Status:DBInstanceStatus}'

# Check ALB subnets (must be ≥ 2 AZs)
aws elbv2 describe-load-balancers \
  --names my-alb \
  --query 'LoadBalancers[0].AvailabilityZones[*].{AZ:ZoneName,SubnetId:SubnetId}' \
  --output table

# Verify ElastiCache Multi-AZ
aws elasticache describe-replication-groups \
  --replication-group-id my-redis \
  --query 'ReplicationGroups[0].{MultiAZ:MultiAZ,NodeGroups:NodeGroups[*].NodeGroupMembers[*].PreferredAvailabilityZone}'
AZ Isolation vs. Equivalent Constructs in Other Clouds
Cloud providers use different terminology and guarantees for their HA zones. Comparing them helps when designing hybrid or multi-cloud architectures:
Separate power, cooling, networking; ≥ 10 km apart
Zones numbered 1–3 (logical, like AWS); zone pinning differs by subscription
Single data-center group; region = collection of zones
Zones may be closer together; lower intra-region RTT but tighter geographic radius
Extension of a region, lower latency, not a full AZ
Limited services; not suitable as a primary HA pair
Compute only; not a redundancy target for core workloads

A VPC subnet is confined to exactly one AZ. When you create a VPC, AWS does not automatically create subnets — you choose the number of subnets, their CIDR blocks, and which AZ each lives in. Best-practice structure for a three-tier application:

# Create a VPC with public and private subnets in three AZs
VPC_ID=$(aws ec2 create-vpc --cidr-block 10.0.0.0/16 \
  --query 'Vpc.VpcId' --output text)
aws ec2 modify-vpc-attribute --vpc-id "$VPC_ID" --enable-dns-support
aws ec2 modify-vpc-attribute --vpc-id "$VPC_ID" --enable-dns-hostnames

# Public subnets (one per AZ)
for i in 0 1 2; do
  AZ="us-east-1$(echo abcdefg | cut -c$((i+1)))"
  aws ec2 create-subnet \
    --vpc-id "$VPC_ID" \
    --cidr-block "10.0.${i}.0/24" \
    --availability-zone "$AZ" \
    --tag-specifications "ResourceType=subnet,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=public-${AZ}},{Key=Tier,Value=public}]"
done

# Private app subnets (one per AZ)
for i in 0 1 2; do
  AZ="us-east-1$(echo abcdefg | cut -c$((i+1)))"
  aws ec2 create-subnet \
    --vpc-id "$VPC_ID" \
    --cidr-block "10.0.$((i+10)).0/24" \
    --availability-zone "$AZ" \
    --tag-specifications "ResourceType=subnet,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=private-${AZ}},{Key=Tier,Value=app}]"
done

echo "VPC $VPC_ID: 6 subnets across 3 AZs created"
NAT Gateway per AZ: Avoiding Cross-AZ NAT Traffic
A single NAT Gateway in one AZ is a common cost-saving mistake. Private-subnet instances in other AZs must route outbound traffic to the NAT Gateway across AZ boundaries, which:
($0.01/GB each way) to all outbound internet traffic.
for outbound internet access: if that AZ is impaired, instances in other AZs lose internet connectivity.
The recommended pattern is one NAT Gateway per AZ, each with its own Elastic IP, and separate route tables that direct
to the local AZ's NAT Gateway:

# Allocate EIPs and create one NAT GW per AZ
for i in 0 1 2; do
  AZ="us-east-1$(echo abc | cut -c$((i+1)))"
  # Get the public subnet for this AZ
  SUBNET=$(aws ec2 describe-subnets \
    --filters "Name=vpc-id,Values=$VPC_ID" \
             "Name=availabilityZone,Values=$AZ" \
             "Name=tag:Tier,Values=public" \
    --query 'Subnets[0].SubnetId' --output text)

  EIP=$(aws ec2 allocate-address --domain vpc --query 'AllocationId' --output text)
  NGW=$(aws ec2 create-nat-gateway --subnet-id "$SUBNET" \
          --allocation-id "$EIP" \
          --tag-specifications "ResourceType=natgateway,Tags=[{Key=AZ,Value=$AZ}]" \
          --query 'NatGateway.NatGatewayId' --output text)

  echo "AZ $AZ: NAT GW $NGW (EIP alloc $EIP)"
done
AWS Resilience Hub: Continuous AZ Resilience Assessment
AWS Resilience Hub analyzes your application's CloudFormation, Terraform, or resource-group definition and produces a
with actionable recommendations. It checks whether each tier of your application meets an RTO/RPO policy.
: Define RTO and RPO targets (e.g., RTO < 60 s, RPO = 0).
: Resilience Hub maps your resources, identifies single-AZ dependencies, and calculates the achievable RTO/RPO.
: Specific resource changes — "add a read replica in us-east-1c", "enable Multi-AZ on this RDS instance", "add target group for 1b subnet".
: Re-run assessments on each deploy to catch new single-AZ bottlenecks introduced by infrastructure-as-code changes.

# Add an application to AWS Resilience Hub (using CloudFormation stack as source)
APP_ARN=$(aws resiliencehub create-app \
  --name my-web-app \
  --assessment-schedule Daily \
  --policy-arn "arn:aws:resiliencehub:us-east-1:123456789012:resiliency-policy/my-sla-policy" \
  --query 'app.appArn' --output text)

# Import resources from a CloudFormation stack
aws resiliencehub import-resources-to-draft-app-version \
  --app-arn "$APP_ARN" \
  --source-arns "arn:aws:cloudformation:us-east-1:123456789012:stack/prod-stack/abc123"

# Publish the app version and run an assessment
aws resiliencehub publish-app-version --app-arn "$APP_ARN"

ASSESSMENT_ARN=$(aws resiliencehub start-app-assessment \
  --app-arn "$APP_ARN" \
  --app-version release \
  --assessment-name baseline-az-check \
  --query 'assessment.assessmentArn' --output text)

echo "Assessment running: $ASSESSMENT_ARN"
# Wait, then fetch results
aws resiliencehub describe-app-assessment \
  --assessment-arn "$ASSESSMENT_ARN" \
  --query 'assessment.{Score:resiliencyScore,Status:assessmentStatus,RTO:estimatedCostTier}'
Runbook: Recovering from an AZ Impairment
When CloudWatch alarms fire for a specific AZ, the following step-by-step runbook keeps MTTR (Mean Time to Recovery) low. Each step includes the CLI command to execute:
: Check the AWS Service Health Dashboard and your per-AZ CloudWatch metrics to confirm whether all services in the AZ are affected or only a subset.
Drain the impaired AZ from load balancers
: Remove the impaired AZ's subnets from ALB/NLB listeners so no new requests are routed there.
: Increase ASG desired capacity in the remaining AZs to absorb the lost capacity.
: For RDS Multi-AZ, confirm automatic failover completed. For Aurora, verify the new primary is in a healthy AZ.
: Any EC2-associated EIPs in the impaired AZ must be disassociated and re-associated to instances in healthy AZs.
: Open an incident, capture impacted resource list, set recovery ETA based on AWS health-event timeline.
: Once AWS declares the AZ healthy, re-enable it gradually (weighted DNS or staged capacity return) to prevent thundering-herd.

# Step 2: drain impaired AZ from ALB (remove subnet, keep others)
aws elbv2 set-subnets \
  --load-balancer-arn "$ALB_ARN" \
  --subnets "$SUBNET_1B" "$SUBNET_1C"   # omit 1a

# Step 3: scale out ASG in healthy AZs
aws autoscaling update-auto-scaling-group \
  --auto-scaling-group-name web-asg \
  --desired-capacity 12   # was 9; 12 across remaining 2 AZs

# Step 4: verify RDS failover completed
aws rds describe-events \
  --source-identifier prod-db \
  --source-type db-instance \
  --duration 60 \
  --query 'Events[?contains(Message, `failover`) || contains(Message, `Failover`)].{Time:Date,Msg:Message}' \
  --output table

# Step 7: re-enable AZ after recovery
aws elbv2 set-subnets \
  --load-balancer-arn "$ALB_ARN" \
  --subnets "$SUBNET_1A" "$SUBNET_1B" "$SUBNET_1C"   # restore all three
Running across multiple AZs has real cost implications. Understanding them ensures no budget surprises:
Only for traffic that crosses AZ boundaries
Standby runs continuously and is billed at full rate
Replica billed same as primary node type
ALB itself is not AZ-multiplied; targets are

The cross-AZ data-transfer charge is the most frequently overlooked. For a service processing 10 TB/month of inter-service traffic, moving from single-AZ to multi-AZ without AZ-affinity routing could add
alone. Use ECS Service Connect with AZ-aware routing, EKS topology hints, or ElastiCache preferred-AZ settings to mitigate this.
(≥ 10 km), independent power substations, dedicated cooling, and separate border routers make each AZ a true fault domain.
adds a software-level isolation layer: no operator access and per-card hardware isolation bound blast radius to the affected host.
: cross-AZ traffic costs $0.01/GB per direction. Design services to minimize cross-AZ calls while maintaining redundancy.
: chaos experiments that terminate all instances in one AZ are the only reliable way to verify your multi-AZ architecture actually works.
are essential: aggregate metrics hide AZ-specific degradation until it is too late to route traffic away gracefully.
